does: This paper develops an adaptive margin framework for reward modeling in RLHF by learning to infer preference strengths from ordinal annotations rather than scalar ratings. The core method uses a hierarchy of preferences—comparisons between preferences themselves—to dynamically adjust margins during training, where stronger preferences receive larger margins between chosen and rejected responses. Rather than fixing margins or deriving them unreliably from noisy preference ratings, the approach trains a model to predict which preference comparisons represent stronger distinctions, using this signal to assign adaptive per-datapoint margins that improve both generalization and robustness of the learned reward model.
connects: This work builds on margin-based learning frameworks long established in supervised learning and support vector machines, adapting them to the RLHF setting where reward models are trained on pairwise preferences. It responds to limitations in existing RLHF methods that either ignore margin structure, use uniform fixed margins, or naively derive margins from preference ratings without accounting for their reliability. The paper extends recent work on preference-based learning by recognizing that preference strength itself varies and deserves modeling. It connects to broader research on learning from noisy or imprecise human feedback by proposing ordinal preference annotations as a more reliable signal than scalar ratings, sidestepping the psychometric challenges of eliciting consistent numerical judgments from humans.
enables: This approach opens pathways toward more sample-efficient and robust RLHF pipelines that can better handle realistic human annotation scenarios where preference elicitation is noisy or uncertain. By leveraging ordinal structure over scalar ratings, the method enables collection protocols that are cognitively easier for annotators, potentially scaling human feedback to larger models. The adaptive margin mechanism points toward future work on learning domain-specific or context-dependent margin structures, mixing preference hierarchies with other signals like uncertainty estimates, and applying similar ordinal preference reasoning to other aspects of alignment beyond reward modeling, such as value learning or multi-objective preference aggregation.
